mechanism_narrative: A sustained decline in the federal funds rate below 2.0% signals monetary accommodation that reduces the discount rate applied to equity valuations, producing positive S&P 500 forward returns over the next 20 trading days as investors rebalance into equities.
candidate_trigger: FEDFUNDS falls below 2.0% and remains there for at least 2 consecutive months
failure_condition: Walk-forward Rank IC remains below 0.03 over any 2-year rolling window, or 20-day forward returns in easing periods are not significantly higher than baseline (t-test p > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: Month-over-month payroll employment growth above 200k signals robust labor demand and consumer spending capacity, driving positive S&P 500 returns over 5 trading days as investors anticipate earnings growth from increased household consumption.
failure_condition: Payroll growth above 200k in month t does not produce positive 5-day returns in > 55% of instances over a 3-year rolling window, or Rank IC < 0.02.

mechanism_narrative: A joint contraction in retail sales (RSAFS) and housing starts (HOUST) signals weakening aggregate demand and consumer confidence, producing negative S&P 500 forward returns over 60 trading days as investors anticipate earnings downgrades and recession risk.
candidate_trigger: RSAFS declines > 2% month-over-month AND HOUST falls > 5% month-over-month simultaneously
failure_condition: Joint RSAFS and HOUST contractions do not precede negative 60-day returns in > 60% of triggered instances, or Rank IC < 0.04 over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Industrial production growth above 1% month-over-month indicates strengthening manufacturing capacity utilization and supply-side expansion, producing positive S&P 500 returns over 20 trading days as investors price in margin expansion and capital expenditure cycles.
failure_condition: INDPRO growth > 1.0% does not produce positive 20-day returns in > 58% of instances, or Rank IC < 0.03 over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Unemployment rate rising above 4.5% signals labor market deterioration and reduced consumer spending power, producing negative S&P 500 forward returns over 60 trading days as investors anticipate earnings pressure from lower household demand and potential recession.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE rises above 4.5% and remains elevated for 2+ consecutive months
regime_hint: labor market deterioration or early recession
failure_condition: UNRATE > 4.5% does not precede negative 60-day returns in > 60% of instances, or Rank IC < 0.04 over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Rising inflation (CPIAUCSL) combined with elevated federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS > 3.0%) signals monetary tightening that compresses equity valuations through higher discount rates and reduced real purchasing power, producing negative S&P 500 returns over 20 trading days.
failure_condition: Joint condition of elevated FEDFUNDS and rising CPIAUCSL does not produce negative 20-day returns in > 62% of triggered instances, or Rank IC < 0.05 over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Real GDP growth (GDPC1) acceleration above 2.5% annualized signals robust economic expansion and earnings growth, producing positive S&P 500 forward returns over 60 trading days as investors increase equity allocations in response to improved growth outlook.
failure_condition: GDPC1 growth > 2.5% does not produce positive 60-day returns in > 60% of instances, or Rank IC < 0.04 over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Housing starts (HOUST) declining below 1000k units combined with retail sales (RSAFS) stagnation signals demand-side weakness and consumer retrenchment, producing negative S&P 500 returns over 5 trading days as investors immediately reprrice earnings expectations downward.
candidate_trigger: HOUST < 1000k units AND RSAFS month-over-month change < 0.2%
failure_condition: Joint condition of low HOUST and stagnant RSAFS does not produce negative 5-day returns in > 58% of triggered instances, or Rank IC < 0.03 over any 2-year rolling window.
